summary: New York City's juvenile detention population has climbed steadily since Fiscal 2022. ACS's own performance reports attribute recent growth to a rise in arrests (including gun-violence cases) and to youth staying in detention much longer while their cases move through court — average length of stay grew from about 38 days in Fiscal 2021 to roughly 60 days in early Fiscal 2026. The longer-running increase also traces back to New York State's 2017 "Raise the Age" law and 2019 bail-reform changes, which shifted an older population facing more serious charges into the city's two juvenile detention centers, according to a city Department of Investigation report.

sources.label: ACS, Fiscal 2026 Preliminary Mayor's Management Report
CBS News New York, "New report details impact of bail reform at NYC juvenile detention centers"

oneLiner: increasing, tied to more arrests, longer stays, and 2017-19 justice reforms
